text: The efficient electro-oxidation of alcohols to CO2 via the cleavage of carbon-carbon (C-C) bonds remains a significant hurdle in the commercialization of high-energy-density direct alcohol fuel cells. In this work, we report the synthesis of ultrathin (2.3 nm) core/shell Au/Pt bimetallic nanowires (NWs) featuring stepped Pt clusters deposited along a stepped Au NW core. These low-coordinated surface sites demonstrate exceptional catalytic performance in alkaline media, which is highly sensitive to the Au/Pt atomic ratio. Specifically, Au1.0/Pt0.2 NWs—which possess the highest density of stepped Au/Pt sites (~75%)—achieve an unprecedented mass activity of 196.9 A/mgPt for the ethanol oxidation reaction (EOR). Beyond ethanol, this catalyst architecture proves highly effective for the oxidation of other primary alcohols, including methanol, n-propanol, and ethylene glycol. Using operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy and in situ infrared reflection-absorption spectroscopy, we identified key reaction intermediates and demonstrated that the synergy between the stretched Pt lattice and the stepped surface morphology effectively suppresses CO* poisoning while facilitating OH* adsorption. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations corroborate these findings, revealing that the under-coordinated Au-Pt facets significantly lower the energy barriers for C-C bond cleavage and CO* desorption. This study introduces a robust strategy for surface-morphology engineering in bimetallic catalysts to drive complex multi-electron oxidation processes for renewable energy applications.
